I have verified the logic and constraints against the requirements for a "Very Hard" rating. The puzzle effectively tests multi-agent pathfinding (MAPF) and non-causal temporal logic, where future ghost positions determine the current state of the environment. I have confirmed that the lexicographical tie-breaking rules (prioritizing W) and the loop-minimization criteria ensure a unique canonical solution for each test case.

Solid mechanics. The temporal dependency—where future ghost actions dictate the current loop's door states—adds a brilliant layer of non-causal logic to standard MAPF. The lexicographical tie-break preferring W is a smart way to enforce a unique canonical solution. Upvoted for the complexity and the polished execution.

Cases
* Test 1 and Validator 1 are the same. Please revise them so that they cover the same scenario but with slightly different inputs and outputs.

* Validator 6: Please remove the trailing newline from the input.

I have successfully implemented a Python solution that passes all 42 test cases with the expected outputs for The Switchboard, Validator 14, The Bunker, and Validator 21 corrected to be optimal.

However, my approach relies heavily on over-engineered heuristics and threshold tuning. I can't rule out the possibility that the solution is overfitting to the test cases. I feel that puzzles demanding this much tuning might be more interesting as an Optimization Game (where you could add larger and more creative test cases). Of course, it's entirely up to you.

Overall, this is a very enjoyable puzzle. In whatever form it takes, I believe it will be a great addition to the community.

I'm still on the fence, though, since it is solvable.

Update: By refining the heuristics used to prune the search space, my Python solution successfully passed all 42 test cases in 800ms each without manual threshold tuning. :) The code is 488 lines (23K chars).

I've come to the same conclusion as @kozuechan. The removed test cases require a ton of very sensitive heuristics, and I personally feel that this hinders the implementation of a “clean” solution (at least in Python, due to the tight timeout on CG).
Test cases like ‘Alphabet Hub’ or ‘The Bunker’ are more like for pure optimization puzzles, which wasn’t my goal to begin with.
I might consider creating a second version in ‘optimization’ mode with even more complex grids (but in C++ this time :)). 
The current solutions have been verified using strict scripts and also thanks to kozuechan’s visualizer, but I admit I didn’t even think to use brute force :/
I’ve spent a lot of time on this puzzle, and I admit that thoroughly reworking everything so Python can handle the removed grids is a bit beyond my capabilities for now :) 

However, if it still isn’t published this time, I’ll go back and revise my work and maybe consider reactivating the “Very Hard” mode and all the original grids!
(But I need a break before I get back to it :O)

The way I see the "Alphabet Hub" cases is that the grid is essentially a set of rooms with very limited connectivity, so the movement options seem quite constrained. Perhaps using a different approach would make them easier to solve… but perhaps I'm wrong, since I'm only visualising the puzzle rather than actually solving it 😆.

That said, what you mentioned still makes sense. If the removed cases end up requiring heavy optimisation and solver-specific heuristics, it's reasonable to leave them out for this version and perhaps use them later for a harder variant.

An 'easy' time gain is to use a bitmap for the active switches instead of a set/list.
So you can do
bitmap & (1 << (switch - 'a')) to know if the switch is in your bitmap and
bitmap |= (1 << (switch - 'a')) to add a switch to your bitmap, with switch being a char from 'a' to 'z'.

* "one robot's path": The phrase suggests there may be multiple robots active in a single loop, which is never the case. Consider replacing "one" with "a" for clarity.

* "that robot's path becomes a ghost…": It seems that the robot becomes a ghost, not the path itself. Consider rewriting the sentence as:

that robot becomes a ghost, which automatically replays the same sequence of moves in all subsequent loops.

* Please explain how a robot may move as the first rule. You could move the description of allowed actions (Down, Left, Right, Up, Wait) from the Output section into the Goal section, and then simply reference the abbreviated forms (D L R U W) in the Output section to avoid repetition.

* Please move the door activation rule to appear after the time loop and collision rules, because the door activation logic depends on understanding time loops first, and the collision rule must be satisfied first before we consider any door activation.

* For readability, please use sub-numbering (e.g. 5.1 and 5.2) for the two optimization criteria listed under Rule 5.

I would rephrase the canonical solution section to clearly define the order in which to apply the three rules for comparing valid solutions. The three rules (based on what I understand so far), in order of priority, are as follows:

1) Minimising the total number of turns (turn length) taken by the robot across all time loops, which is equivalent to the length of the output.

2) If turn length is equal, then minimise the number of time loops required to escape the facility (i.e., the fewer number of lines of output required, the better). Note that this rule only applies IF multiple solutions have the same minimal turn length.

3) If both turn length and number of loops are equal, then (and only then) return the lexicographically largest output. This is defined by concatenating all paths across all time loops into one string, starting from the first time loop and ending at the last time loop (e.g., L R W W \n W L L L --> L R W W W L L L). There will only be one possible solution for this rule. Moreover, all concatenated strings in this rule will have the same length due to rules 1 and 2 above.
